methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $40M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Forge Nano is a Series C-stage climate and energy company that has raised $40M to date. The valuation analysis triangulates across a peer set of 3 private companies operating in the same sector and stage: KoBold Metals ($2.0B), Electric Hydrogen ($1.0B), and Amogy ($700M), alongside a broader comparable universe of 9 private firms and 1 public company in the climate and energy space.

Two methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis uses the company's $40M total raised as a base value proxy and applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical for Series C advancement, yielding $68M. The peer-median method references the three same-sector, same-stage comparables, which exhibit a median valuation of $1.0B with a range from $700M to $2.0B.

The triangulated analysis produces a range of $58M (low) to $1.1B (high), with a median of $534M. The wide dispersion reflects material uncertainty around the applicability of larger-valuation peers—KoBold and Electric Hydrogen may benefit from differentiated technology positions, strategic backing, or advanced deployment stages not directly comparable to Forge Nano. The stage-progression method produces a more conservative result, while peer-median valuation is anchored to market transactions but exhibits substantial heterogeneity. Key uncertainties include relative technology readiness, capital intensity of commercialization, and customer concentration.
